chapters.title: Anger is a waste of your hours
chapters.text:
A nation's history never runs in a straight line, and it is shaped by complicated people. If you had met Ruth Bader Ginsburg, known as RBG, in her younger years or later on, you might not have given her a second look. She never tried to stand out and kept to herself. Yet this quiet woman made history many times over. Only the second woman ever nominated to the Supreme Court, she argued and won several women's rights cases before the bench. One unfair case at a time, she laid the groundwork for the more equal society we live in today. Each step forward was a hard-won gain, but those obstacles never stopped her. Her principles guided her through every fight.

The arc of the moral universe is long, but it bends toward justice, as Ruth Bader Ginsburg said.

Her mother's advice also helped her along the way. Anger is never worth the time it costs. She learned to accept the range of views and personalities around her and to work toward fair compromises.

Resentment, anger, and other heavy feelings are never useful. They just burn up time and energy.

Ruth Bader Ginsburg was not only an expert in her field. She was, first and foremost, a kind and caring person. Those personal qualities were what made her such a fair and professional judge. She was reserved, took her time, and chose every word with care.

This short summary will help you rediscover RBG by sketching a full picture of an outstanding person, both in her work and in her character. We will walk through her hard road to the Supreme Court and the qualities that made it possible.

chapters.title: Blocking out the noise and pushing through
chapters.text:
As a young Jewish girl from Brooklyn, Ruth Bader Ginsburg attended James Madison High School and graduated in 1950. No one in those classrooms could have predicted that quiet Ruth would grow up to be a Supreme Court Justice. That kind of role was thought to be for boys.

Ruth read Greek and Norse myths and the adventures of the fearless Nancy Drew. Her childhood was happy, thanks to her strict but loving mother, Celia Amster. When Celia fell gravely ill, RBG worked and studied harder than ever to make her proud. It paid off. She graduated with honors and went on to Cornell University. She never made it to her high school graduation, because her mother died only days before. That loss did not slow her down. It drove her to become the accomplished woman her mother could have been if society had given her the chance.

At Cornell, RBG majored in Government and took an undergraduate class in Constitutional Law. That class lit the spark for her life's work in law.

Cornell also gave her something else. She met a fellow student, Marty Ginsburg, and married him in 1954. He became her partner and supporter for life. Together they headed to Harvard Law School. While there, RBG picked up another key lesson, this time from her mother-in-law. She advised her to pretend to be a little hard of hearing and to tune out the cruel things people said. That skill helped RBG at Cornell, at Harvard, and later at Fort Sill. Women in law programs were rare, and the few who were there often hid how sharp they were. RBG did the same for a while. At Harvard, she was one of only two women on the Law Review, a student-run journal of legal scholarship. That was a rare honor at the time.

Sometimes it is smarter to ignore unkind people. That way, they cannot steal your peace.

As a student, RBG ran into shocking levels of sex discrimination. She was once told she could not enter a reading room that was reserved for men. She was barred from the Annual Law Review Banquet because it was a boys-only event. Perhaps the worst sign of all was that the main exam building did not have a women's restroom. None of this angered or scared her. She just kept working.

chapters.title: Grit and steady nerves carry you to the top
chapters.text:
Before Harvard, RBG and Marty spent two years at the Fort Sill army base after Marty was called up to active duty. While there, RBG sat the civil service exam for a federal job, but the offer vanished once the panel learned she was three months pregnant. The wall of rules she hit at Fort Sill lit a new fire in her. When she came back, she studied with a young child at home. On top of that, Marty was diagnosed with testicular cancer, so for a long stretch, RBG was reading briefs and grading papers for two people at once.

After finishing near the top of her class, RBG had a few strong offers on the table. Her Harvard mentor, Professor Al Sacks, put her name forward for a clerkship with Supreme Court Justice Frankfurter. Frankfurter had never rejected a Sacks pick before. RBG broke that streak, and she figured she had three marks against her. She was a woman, a Jew, and a mother.

Job hunting was a maze. Many interview sign-up sheets had men only at the top.

A break came when Gerald Gunther, a professor at Columbia Law School who valued her mind and her work ethic, lined up a District Court clerkship for her. RBG threw herself into the role under Federal Judge Edmund L. Palmieri of the Southern District of New York. Not long after, an unusual chance landed in her lap, an invitation to co-write a book on Swedish civil procedure. RBG kissed her husband and daughter goodbye for a while and moved across the Atlantic. That stretch of independence stayed with her forever, because it let her stand on her own two feet and showed her a world where women had more say in their own lives.

chapters.title: Equality for women needs strong men
chapters.text:
A year before her first Supreme Court argument, RBG and a small group of like-minded colleagues set up the ACLU's Women's Rights Project. Through that work, RBG got her hands on the case of Frontiero v. Richardson and brought it to the Supreme Court. In the case, she argued that benefits given to military service members' spouses could not be handed out differently based on the gender of the service member.

Usually quiet and unsure of herself, RBG shifted gears during her oral argument. She came across as confident, clear, and persuasive. She knew the case better than the judges did, and it showed. She spoke for nearly ten minutes without interruption. Her careful arguments and well-chosen analogies won the day.

Once she started volunteering for the ACLU, RBG received a flood of letters from women dealing with everyday sex discrimination. One woman had been discharged from the military because she was pregnant. Another was kept off the varsity team even though she was the best tennis player at her university. Another was turned away from Princeton's summer engineering program, all because of gender. The examples kept piling up.

To RBG's surprise, many female law students did more than complain. They started fighting for their rights. Things began to change, and in the middle of that change, RBG was asked to teach the first-ever class on women in law.

Around that time, RBG realized she could no longer stay silent. Quiet acceptance was no longer an option. She and her female colleagues filed a federal pay-discrimination claim against the university where she worked. They won. From then on, she was a regular at the Supreme Court, fighting case after case for equality and an end to discrimination.

Real, lasting change in our society happens slowly, one step at a time.

RBG worked tactically. She introduced one careful logical step at a time so the court could keep up. She believed that pushing too fast would wreck the progress she had made.

It is worth noting that RBG never once argued that women deserved special treatment or that they were better than men. Her case was for equal treatment on both sides. She wanted a world where men and women could each reach their full potential as human beings.

chapters.title: A good marriage is a partnership that runs both ways
chapters.text:
Marty Ginsburg never minded living in the shadow of his famous wife. For one thing, he was highly successful in his own field. For another, his support for RBG was unconditional and genuine. One could argue that more than fifty years of a happy marriage, full of warmth and steady romance, is as much of an achievement as breaking through a glass ceiling.

Marty and Ruth adored each other, and that love let them chase their own dreams without giving up on family life. Friends of the couple often pointed out how similar yet different they were. Where RBG was serious and quiet, Marty was playful and funny. He brought out a side of her that no one else could.

For RBG, her marriage was a model of mutual respect and equality between men and women. No matter how rough the legal world was to her, she always got first-class treatment at home.

A strong marriage lets both partners keep their independence while sharing life and goals side by side.

That kind of marriage was rare for her generation. Marty, even though he did not seek the spotlight, became a quiet role model for men ready to change. He was selfless, loving, and respectful of his wife's choices, far from the old stereotypes of toxic masculinity. When he spoke about his relationship with RBG, he said their mutual support from the start had never been a sacrifice. It was simply part of how their family worked.

chapters.title: Welcome views that differ from your own
chapters.text:
It is hard to imagine what was going through RBG's mind when the Oval Office called, after she had nearly given up hope of being nominated to the Supreme Court. Was she scared? Maybe excited? Nervous? Later, she joked that she felt like a bride. All eyes were on her, and she was about to start a unique and exciting journey.

Even though the list of possible nominees was long, on June 14, 1993, the day President Bill Clinton announced that RBG was his pick, no one could miss the certainty in his voice. He called her a legal star and a hero of the women's movement, a moderate centrist with strong morals.

Ruth Bader Ginsburg cannot be called a liberal or a conservative. She has proved herself too thoughtful for such simple labels, as Bill Clinton said.

RBG's appointment to the Supreme Court raised the visibility of women's talents across society.

On the Court, RBG was as steady and rational as ever. She looked for compromise and never let politics or personal views get in the way of her relationships with the other Justices.

Even though sex discrimination was no longer as common as it once was, it was still a huge problem, and RBG now had to fight it in a very different way.

As a Justice, Ruth Bader Ginsburg never forgot her principles. She wrote sharp, powerful dissents. When two more women joined the Court during the Obama administration, she did all she could to help them feel safe and confident.

A surprising note from her life: RBG's closest friend on the bench was Justice Scalia, whose views were as far from hers as you could get.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
In her later years, after the painful loss of her husband Marty to cancer in 2010, RBG never lost heart. She kept looking for the joy in life and kept working. The progress she had fought for was real, but the struggle was not over. Today, much of what RBG fought for is still at risk. To honor her life's work and her priceless legacy, we have to keep doing what Ruth Bader Ginsburg started.

Ruth Bader Ginsburg is a fine example of a modern hero who fights with carefully chosen words and sharp thinking, not with her fists. After facing discrimination herself, she spent her whole life challenging injustice and making the country fairer for everyone. Much like her mother was taken from her too early, RBG was taken from us while society still needed her wisdom and strong principles. Justice Ginsburg died in 2020 at the age of 87 from cancer. That is exactly why it falls on us now to challenge the inequalities that remain and keep fighting discrimination.

To live a little more like RBG, keep her fight for equality and fair justice alive in your own corner of the world. Always pick your battles, since anger gets in the way, so choose the fights that are truly worth your time and energy. Do not burn bridges. The fight for what is right cannot be won alone, so be the kind of leader who pulls others in. And invest time in your marriage, since a healthy romantic relationship can give you real support in your work and your growth as a person.
